title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
#Getting started
There is a need to check if we have installed the relevant packages.

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes, 
               tidyverse)

Import and read the data set, which is the Exam_data.csv file

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

Making different plots using ggplot.
First plot is to compare the English and Maths scores for Primary 3.

#ggplot(data=exam_data,
#       aes(x=MATHS,
#           y=ENGLISH))
#+ geom_point() 
#+ geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5,
#              vjust= -.5)
#+ coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
#                  ylim=c(0,100))

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Getting started
There is a need to check if we have installed the relevant packages.

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes, 
               tidyverse)

Import and read the data set, which is the Exam_data.csv file

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")



Making different plots using ggplot.
First plot is to compare the English and Maths scores for Primary 3.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS,
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5,
              vjust= -.5)+ 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))+ 
  ggtitle("English scores versus Math scores for Primary 3")








Second plot is to compare the Science and Maths scores for Primary 3.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS,
           y=SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5,
              vjust= -.5)+ 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))+ 
  ggtitle("English scores versus Math scores for Primary 3")








I am going to plot the same type of graph to compare race and Maths scores.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=RACE,
           y=SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5,
              vjust= -.5)+ 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))+ 
  ggtitle("English scores versus Math scores for Primary 3")








This plot appears strange as RACE is a categorical data type. Hence, the few values of RACE are compressed to the far left, making it difficult to interpret.
A histogram or bar chart would be a better choice to plot a categorical and numerical into one plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=SCIENCE))+
  geom_histogram(bins=25,
                 boundary=100,
                 color="grey25",
                 fill="grey90")+
  ggtitle("Distribution of Science scores")+
  theme_economist()








This plot is much easier to interpret. It is important to take note of data types when deciding what plots,charts and graphs to use.
I will make another plot to see the count of students by race. However, the code below will give an error as geom_histogram() is for continuous numeric data, but RACE is a categorical/discrete variable.That is why ggplot says:stat_bin() requires a continuous x aesthetic.

#ggplot(data=exam_data,
#       aes(x=RACE))+
#  geom_histogram(bins=25,
#                 boundary=100,
#                 color="grey25",
#                fill="grey90")+
#  ggtitle("Distribution of Science scores")+
#  theme_economist()

Instead, we can use geom_bar().

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar(color = "grey25",
           fill = "grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Race") +
  theme_economist()










Using ggrepel:

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")










Working with ggtheme package:

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()









ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()









ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")










Combining more than one graph into a single display:
Here we will assign the individual plots to a variable.

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
          aes(x=MATHS))+
      geom_histogram(bins=20,
                     boundary=100,
                     color="grey25",
                     fill="grey90")+
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100))+
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths Score")


p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
             aes(x=ENGLISH))+
  geom_histogram(bins=20,
                 boundary=100,
                 color="blue2",
                 fill="white")+
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100))+
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")


p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

Now, combine the 2 plots into one.

p1 + p2








Lets combine 3 into one.

p1+p2+p3








In the above plot, some portions of the title are cut out. So, we increase the width and height of the area to plot.

p1 + p2 + p3








There are other ways to display the charts into 1.
“/” operator to stack two ggplot2 graphs, “|” operator to place the plots beside each other, “()” operator the define the sequence of the plotting.

(p1 / p2) | p3








tagging/numbering:

((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')








Inserting the plot at specific locations/areas:

p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)









patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()










Alternatives

Label only selected points instead of every point


nrow(subset(exam_data, MATHS > 80 | ENGLISH > 80))

[1] 117

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_label_repel(
    data = subset(exam_data, MATHS > 80 | ENGLISH > 80),
    aes(label = ID),
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for P3")









Use geom_text_repel()


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = ID),
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 3
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for P3")









Map color to a grouping variable in the labelled scatterplot


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH, colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5, se = FALSE) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = subset(exam_data, MATHS > 85 | ENGLISH > 85),
    aes(label = ID),
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English V Maths scores by Gender")









Using a different theme instead of ggthemes. Use theme_wsj()


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(
    bins = 20,
    boundary = 100,
    color = "grey25",
    fill = "grey90"
  ) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_fivethirtyeight()









Use theme()


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(
    bins = 20,
    boundary = 100,
    color = "grey25",
    fill = "grey90"
  ) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 13),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11)
  )









Combine 2 plots with wrap_plots()


wrap_plots(p1, p2, ncol = 2)









Adding overall title and subtitle


(p1 / p2) | p3 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Exam Performance",
    subtitle = "Distribution of Maths & English scores with relationship plot",
    tag_levels = "A"
  )

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, 
               patchwork, DT, tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
using ggiraph package

The plotThe code











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE, 
    binwidth = 1, 
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, 
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Style 1

The plotThe code











tooltip_css <- "background-color:white; #<<
font-style:bold; color:black;" #<<

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = ID),                   
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(    #<<
    opts_tooltip(    #<<
      css = tooltip_css)) #<<
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
Hands-on Exercise 3
Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Displaying median instead of mean

The plotThe code











tooltip <- function(y, ymin, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  median_val <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Median maths scores:", median_val)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE)) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS,
        tooltip = after_stat(
          tooltip(y, ymin, ymax)
        )),
    fun = median,                  
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill = "lightblue"
  ) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = median_hilow,       
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    size = 0.2
  )

girafe(
  ggobj = gg_point,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.618
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
section: Displaying standard deviation instead of mean
text: Displaying standard deviation instead of mean

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Displaying standard deviation

The plotThe code











mean_sd <- function(x) {
  m <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  s <- sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  data.frame(
    y = m,
    ymin = m - s,
    ymax = m + s
  )
}

tooltip <- function(y, ymin, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean_val <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sd_val <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean_val, "\nSD:", sd_val)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE)) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS,
        tooltip = after_stat(
          tooltip(y, ymin, ymax)
        )),
    fun.data = mean_sd,                 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill = "lightblue"
  ) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_sd,                 
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    size = 0.2
  )

girafe(
  ggobj = gg_point,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.618
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
adding a visual element

The plotThe code











plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
        x = ~ENGLISH, 
        y = ~MATHS, 
        color = ~RACE)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
section: Creating an interactive scatterplot, ggplotly method
text:
Creating an interactive scatterplot, ggplotly method

The plotThe code











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
ggplotly(p)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Coordinated linked plot, using plotly

The plotThe code











d <- highlight_key(exam_data)
p1 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

p2 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
subplot(ggplotly(p1),
        ggplotly(p2))

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Linked Brushing

The plotThe code





















d <- highlight_key(exam_data) 
p <- ggplot(d, 
            aes(ENGLISH, 
                MATHS)) + 
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

gg <- highlight(ggplotly(p),        
                "plotly_selected")  

crosstalk::bscols(gg,               
                  DT::datatable(d), 
                  widths = 5)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Interactive boxplot

The plotThe code











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~RACE,
  y = ~MATHS,
  color = ~RACE,
  type = "box"
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Interactive scatterplot with tooltip

The plotThe code











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~MATHS,
  y = ~ENGLISH,
  color = ~RACE,
  text = ~paste(
    "ID:", ID,
    "<br>Class:", CLASS,
    "<br>Maths:", MATHS,
    "<br>English:", ENGLISH
  ),
  hoverinfo = "text",
  type = "scatter",
  mode = "markers"
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Interactive violin plot

The plotThe code











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~RACE,
  y = ~MATHS,
  color = ~RACE,
  type = "violin",
  box = list(visible = TRUE),
  meanline = list(visible = TRUE)
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Interactive histogram

The plotThe code











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~MATHS,
  color = ~RACE,
  type = "histogram",
  opacity = 0.7
) %>%
  layout(
    barmode = "overlay",
    title = "Interactive Histogram of Maths Scores"
  )

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Interactive density heatmap

The plotThe code











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~MATHS,
  y = ~ENGLISH,
  type = "histogram2d",
  colorscale = "YlOrRd"
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Displaying standard deviation

The plotThe code











mean_sd <- function(x) {
  m <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  s <- sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  data.frame(
    y = m,
    ymin = m - s,
    ymax = m + s
  )
}

tooltip <- function(y, ymin, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean_val <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sd_val <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean_val, "\nSD:", sd_val)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE)) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS,
        tooltip = after_stat(
          tooltip(y, ymin, ymax)
        )),
    fun.data = mean_sd,                 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill = "lightblue"
  ) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_sd,                 
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    size = 0.2
  )

  ggobj = gg_point,

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Animated line chart

The plotThe code














ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Year, y = Population, colour = Country)) +
  geom_line(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Population Growth Over Time: {frame_along}") +
  transition_reveal(Year)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Animated bar chart

The plotThe code














globalPop_continent <- globalPop %>%
  group_by(Year, Continent) %>%
  summarise(Population = sum(Population), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(globalPop_continent,
       aes(x = Continent,
           y = Population,
           fill = Continent)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Year: {frame_time}",
       x = "Continent",
       y = "Population") +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear")

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Animated area chart

The plotThe code














globalPop %>%
  group_by(Year, Continent) %>%
  summarise(Population = sum(Population), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, 
             y = Population, 
             fill = Continent,
             group = Continent)) +
  geom_area(stat = "identity", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Global Population by Continent: {frame_along}") +
  transition_reveal(along = Year)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Animated histogram

The plotThe code














ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Ageing Population: {frame_time}",
    x = "% Aged",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear")

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Animated scatter

The plotThe code














ggplot(globalPop,
       aes(x = Old,
           y = Young,
           colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~Continent) +
  labs(
    title = "Age Structure by Continent: {frame_time}",
    x = "% Aged",
    y = "% Young"
  ) +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear")

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visualising_Distribution
text:
pacman::p_load(ggdist, ggridges, ggthemes, 
               colorspace, tidyverse)

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visualising_Distribution
section: Ridgeline plot or joyplot shows the distribution of a numeric variable across multiple categories or groups. It is useful when you want to compare distributions side by side. For example, population by continent over time, income distribution by country, exam scores by class, temperature distribution by month.
text:
Ridgeline plot or joyplot shows the distribution of a numeric variable across multiple categories or groups. It is useful when you want to compare distributions side by side. For example, population by continent over time, income distribution by country, exam scores by class, temperature distribution by month.

The plotThe code


There are multiple ways to plot ridgeline plot with R. This section, we are using ggridges package.












ggplot(exam, 
       aes(x = ENGLISH, 
           y = CLASS)) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    bandwidth = 3.4,
    fill = lighten("#7097BB", .3),
    color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
    ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visualising_Distribution
section: Varying fill colors along the x axis
text:
Varying fill colors along the x axis
Basically, a gradient effect along the axis.

using geom_density_ridges_gradient

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam, 
       aes(x = ENGLISH, 
           y = CLASS,
           fill = stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Temp. [F]",
                       option = "C") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()






using geom_ridgeline_gradient()

The plotThe code














library(ggplot2)
library(ggridges)

ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           height = after_stat(density),
           fill = after_stat(x))) +
  
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "ridgeline_gradient",
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01
  ) +
  
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "Temp. [F]",
    option = "C"
  ) +
  
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  
  scale_y_discrete(
    name = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  
  theme_ridges()

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visualising_Distribution
section: Alternatively, instead of using numbers to define the 1st, 2nd,3rd and 4th quantile, we can also specify quantiles by cut off points.
text:
Alternatively, instead of using numbers to define the 1st, 2nd,3rd and 4th quantile, we can also specify quantiles by cut off points.
First quartile (0–25%). Second quartile (25–50%). Third quartile (50–75%). Fourth quartile (75–100%).

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH, 
           y = CLASS, 
           fill = factor(stat(quantile))
           )) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE, 
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
    ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("(0, 0.025]", "(0.025, 0.975]", "(0.975, 1]")
  ) +
  theme_ridges()

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visualising_Distribution
text:
Plotting a half eye graph

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam, 
       aes(x = RACE, 
           y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA)

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visualising_Distribution
text:
Adding the boxplot with geom_boxplot()

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam, 
       aes(x = RACE, 
           y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA)

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visualising_Distribution
section: Adding the dot plots with stat_dots()
text:
Adding the dot plots with stat_dots()

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam, 
       aes(x = RACE, 
           y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA) +
  stat_dots(side = "left", 
            justification = 1.2, 
            binwidth = .5,
            dotsize = 2)

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visualising_Distribution
section: Use coord_flip() to flip the raincloud chart
text:
Use coord_flip() to flip the raincloud chart

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam, 
       aes(x = RACE, 
           y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA) +
  stat_dots(side = "left", 
            justification = 1.2, 
            binwidth = .5,
            dotsize = 1.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_economist()

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visualising_Distribution
text: pacman::p_load(ggstatsplot, tidyverse)

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visualising_Distribution
text:
Comparing English scores across Classes
This analysis compares English performance across multiple classes

The plotThe code














ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = CLASS,
  y = ENGLISH,
  type = "np",
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "s",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE
)

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visualising_Distribution
section: Correlation between Math and Science scores
text:
Correlation between Math and Science scores
This checks if students who perform well in Science also tend to perform well in Maths.

The plotThe code














ggscatterstats(
  data = exam,
  x = SCIENCE,
  y = MATHS,
  marginal = TRUE,
  type = "parametric",
  conf.level = 0.95,
  messages = FALSE
)

Association between Race and Gender
This tests whether there is a significant association between Race and Gender. It checks whether 2 categorical variables are related.

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visual_Uncertainty
text:
pacman::p_load(plotly, crosstalk, DT, 
               ggdist, ggridges, colorspace,
               gganimate, tidyverse)

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visual_Uncertainty
text:
stat_pointinterval()

The plotThe code














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE, 
             y = MATHS)) +
  stat_pointinterval() +
  labs(
    title = "Visualising confidence intervals of mean math score",
    subtitle = "Mean Point + Multiple-interval plot")

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visual_Uncertainty
section: adding in arguments to above code
text:
adding in arguments to above code

The plotThe code














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE, y = MATHS)) +
  stat_pointinterval(.width = 0.95,
  .point = median,
  .interval = qi) +
  labs(
    title = "Visualising confidence intervals of median math score",
    subtitle = "Median Point + Multiple-interval plot")

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visual_Uncertainty
text:
using stat_grradientinterval()

The plotThe code














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE, 
             y = MATHS)) +
  stat_gradientinterval(   
    fill = "skyblue",      
    show.legend = TRUE     
  ) +                        
  labs(
    title = "Visualising confidence intervals of mean math score",
    subtitle = "Gradient + interval plot")

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visual_Uncertainty
text:
Violin plot with boxplot overlay
Unlike error bars which only show interval estimates, violin plots reveal: skewness, multimodal distributions, clustering, spread uncertainty.

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = RACE,
           y = MATHS,
           fill = RACE)) +

  geom_violin(alpha = 0.6,
              trim = FALSE) +

  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15,
               outlier.color = "red",
               alpha = 0.7) +

  labs(
    title = "Distribution and uncertainty of Maths scores by race",
    subtitle = "Violin plot with boxplot overlay"
  ) +

  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visual_Uncertainty
text:
Bootstrapped mean confidence intervals

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = RACE,
           y = MATHS,
           color = RACE)) +

  stat_summary(
    fun.data = mean_cl_boot,
    geom = "pointrange"
  ) +

  labs(
    title = "Bootstrapped confidence intervals of Maths scores",
    subtitle = "Mean with bootstrap confidence intervals"
  ) +

  theme_minimal()




In the plot above, it appears blank as the points/error bars are probably very small or hidden due to grouping/ aesthetic issues. Another improved code will be to split geom from pointrange into point and errorbar. This makes the interval and mean point much more visible.

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = RACE,
           y = MATHS)) +
  stat_summary(
    fun.data = mean_cl_boot,
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean,
    geom = "point",
    size = 3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Bootstrapped mean confidence intervals",
    subtitle = "Mean Maths score with bootstrap confidence intervals",
    x = "Race",
    y = "Maths score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visual_Uncertainty
text:
Half eye plot (ggdist)
The code below creates a half-density + interval plot. It simultaneously shows full distribution, median, credible confidence intervals and uncertainty spread. Compared to traditional error bars, half-eye plots show the full uncertainty distribution, reveal skewness, show density concentration and communicate uncertainty more naturally.

The plotThe code














library(ggdist)

exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = MATHS,
             y = RACE,
             fill = RACE)) +

  stat_halfeye(
    .width = c(0.50, 0.95),
    point_interval = median_qi
  ) +

  labs(
    title = "Half-eye plot of Maths score uncertainty",
    subtitle = "Density + interval uncertainty visualisation"
  ) +

  theme_minimal()




The differences between the 3 alternatives would be: 1. Violin + boxplot is best for detecting clustering/skewness and its main focus is distribution shape + spread. 2. Bootstrapped CI is best for non-normal data and its main focus is robust interval estimation. 3. Half-eye plot is best for Bayesian style uncertainty visualisation and its main focus is density + uncertainty together.

title: Hands-on_Ex04_FunnelPlots
text:
Interactive Funnel Plot: plotly +ggplot2

The plotThe code











fp_ggplotly <- ggplotly(p,
  tooltip = c("label", 
              "x", 
              "y"))
fp_ggplotly

title: Hands-on_Ex04_FunnelPlots
text:
Funnel Plot with highlighted outliers
This classified each sub district as above expected, below expected and within expected range.

The plotThe code














df <- covid19 %>%
  mutate(rate = Death / Positive) %>%
  filter(Positive > 0, rate > 0)

overall_rate <- sum(df$Death) / sum(df$Positive)

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    se = sqrt((overall_rate * (1 - overall_rate)) / Positive),
    lower_95 = overall_rate - 1.96 * se,
    upper_95 = overall_rate + 1.96 * se,
    status = case_when(
      rate > upper_95 ~ "Above expected",
      rate < lower_95 ~ "Below expected",
      TRUE ~ "Within expected range"
    )
  )

ggplot(df, aes(x = Positive, y = rate, colour = status)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = overall_rate, linetype = "solid") +
  geom_line(aes(y = lower_95), colour = "grey40", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(aes(y = upper_95), colour = "grey40", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Funnel Plot with Highlighted Outliers",
    subtitle = "Sub-districts outside the 95% control limits are highlighted",
    x = "Cumulative Positive Cases",
    y = "Fatality Rate",
    colour = "Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on_Ex04_FunnelPlots
text:
Interactive labelled funnel plot

The plotThe code











df <- covid19 %>%
  mutate(rate = Death / Positive) %>%
  filter(Positive > 0, rate > 0)

overall_rate <- sum(df$Death) / sum(df$Positive)

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    se = sqrt((overall_rate * (1 - overall_rate)) / Positive),
    lower_95 = overall_rate - 1.96 * se,
    upper_95 = overall_rate + 1.96 * se,
    tooltip = paste(
      "Sub-district:", `Sub-district`,
      "<br>Positive cases:", Positive,
      "<br>Deaths:", Death,
      "<br>Fatality rate:", round(rate, 4)
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = Positive, y = rate, text = tooltip)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = overall_rate, colour = "grey30") +
  geom_line(aes(y = lower_95), colour = "grey40", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(aes(y = upper_95), colour = "grey40", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Interactive Funnel Plot",
    subtitle = "Hover over each point to view sub-district details",
    x = "Cumulative Positive Cases",
    y = "Fatality Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")
